raw_text: REPORTABLE IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION CIVIL APPEAL Nos.823-854 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (C ) Nos. 15919- 15950 of 2011) Kerala Public Service Commission  Ors. ..Appellants Versus The State Information Commission  Anr. .Respondents With CIVIL APPEAL NO.855 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (Civil) No.5433 of 2014) Public Service Commission U.P. ..Appellant Versus Raghvendra Singh .. Respondent J U D G M E N T M.Y. EQBAL, J. Leave granted. 2. In these two appeals the short question which needs consideration is as to whether the Division Bench of the Kerala High Court by impugned judgment has right ly held that the respondents are entitled not only to get information with regard to the scan copies of their answer sheet, 1 561 Page 1 tabulation-sheet containing interview marks but also entitled to know the names of the examiners who have evaluate d the answer sheet. 3. The information sought for by the respondents were denied by the State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority. However, the State Information Commission allowed the second appeal and held that there is no fiduciary rela tionship in case of answer scripts. Further, the interview marks cannot be considered as personal information, since the public authority had already decided to publish them. 4. Both the High Courts of Kerala and Allahabad have taken the view, following th e earlier decisions of this Court that no fiduciary relationship exists between the appellants and the respondents and, therefore, the information sought for have to be supplied to them. 5. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and have gone through the impugned judgments passed by the 2 562 Page 2 Division Bench of the High Court of Kerala at Ernakulam and Allahabad. 6. So far as the information sought for by the respondents with regard to the supply of scanned copies of his answer -sheet of the written test, copy of the tabulation sheet and other information, we are of the opinion that the view taken in the impugned judgment with regard to the disclosure of these information, do not suffer from error of law and the same is fully justified. However, the view of the Kerala High Court is that the information seekers are also entitled to get the disclosure of names of examiners who have evaluated the answer-sheet. 7. The view taken by the Kerala High Court holding that no fiduciary relationship exists b etween the University and the Commission and the examiners appointed by them cannot be sustained in law. The Kerala High Court while observing held:- 16.What, if any , is the fiduciary relationship of the PSC qua the examinees? Performance audit of constitutional institutions would only 3 563 Page 3 strengthen the confidence of the citizenry in such institutions. The PSC is a constitutional institution. To stand above board, is one of its own prime requirements. There is nothing that should deter disclosure of the contents of the materials that the examinees provide as part of their performance in the competition for being selected to public service. The confidence that may be reposed by the examinees in the institution of the PSC does not inspire the acceptability of a fiduciary relationship that should kindle the exclusion of information in relation to the evalution or other details relating to the examination. Once the evaluation is over and results are declared, no more secrecy is called for. D issemination of such information would only add to the credibility of the PSC, in the constitutional conspectus in which it is placed. A particular examinee would therefore be entitled to access to information in relation to that persons answer scripts. A s regards others, information in relation to answer scripts may fall within the pale of third party information in terms of section 11 of the RTI Act. This only means that such information cannot be accessed except in conformity with the provisions conta ined in section 11. It does not, in any manner, provide for any immunity from access. 17. We shall now examine the next contention of PSC that there is a fiduciary relationship between it and the examiners and as a consequence, it is eligible to claim pro tection from disclosure, except with the sanction of the competent authority, as regards the identity of the examiners as also the materials that were subjected to the examination. We have already approved TREESA and the different precedents and commentari es relied on therein as regards the concept of fiduciary relationship. We are in full agreement with the law laid by the Division Bench of this Court in Centre of Earth Science 4 564 Page 4 Studies (supra), that S.8 (1)(e) deals with information available with the person in his fiduciary relationship with another; that information under this head is nothing but information in trust, which, but for the relationship would not have been conveyed or known to the person concerned. What is it that the PS C holds in trust for the examiners? Nothing. At the best, it could be pointed out that the identity of the examiners has to be insulated from public gaze, having regard to issues relatable to vulnerability and exposure to corruption if the identities of th e examiners are disclosed in advance. But, at any rate, such issues would go to oblivion after the conclusion of the evaluation of the answer scripts and the publication of the results. Therefore, it would not be in public interest to hold that there could be a continued secrecy even as regards the identity of the examiners. Access to such information, including as to the identity of the examiners, after the examination and evaluation process are over, cannot be shied off under any law or avowed principle of privacy. 8. We do not find any substance in the reasoning given by the Kerala High Court on the question of disclosure of names of the examiners. 9. In the present case, the PSC has taken upon itself in appointing the examiners to evaluate the answer papers and as such, the PSC and examiners stand in a principal -agent relationship. Here the PSC in the shoes of a Principal 5 565 Page 5 has entrusted the task of evaluating the answer papers to the Examiners. Consequently, Examiners in the positio n of agents are bound to evaluate the answer papers as per the instructions given by the PSC. As a result, a fiduciary relationship is established between the PSC and the Examiners. Therefore, any information shared between them is not liable to be disclos ed. Furthermore, the information seeker has no role to play in this and we dont see any logical reason as to how this will benefit him or the public at large. We would like to point out that the disclosure of the identity of Examiners is in the least inte rest of the general public and also any attempt to reveal the examiners identity will give rise to dire consequences. Therefore, in our considered opinion revealing examiners identity will only lead to confusion and public unrest. Hence, we are not inclined to agree with the decision of the Kerala High Court with respect to the second question. 6 566 Page 6 10. In the present case the request of the information seeker about the information of his answer sheets and details of the interview marks can be and should be provided to him. It is not something which a public authority keeps it under a fiduciary capacity. Even disclosing the marks and the answer sheets to the candidates will ensure that the candidates have been given marks according to their performance in the exam. This practice will ensure a fair play in this competitive environment, where candidate puts his time in preparing for the competitive exams, but, the request of the information seeker about the details of the person who had examinedchecked the paper cannot and shall not be provided to the information seeker as the relationship between the public authority i.e. Service Commission and the Examiners is totally within fiduciary relationship. The Commission has reposed trust on t he examiners that they will check the exam papers with utmost care, honesty and impartially and, similarly, the Examiners have faith that 7 567 Page 7 they will not be facing any unfortunate consequences for doing their job properly. If we allo w disclosing name of the examiners in every exam, the unsuccessful candidates may try to take revenge from the examiners for doing their job properly. This may, further, create a situation where the potential candidates in the next similar exam, especially in the same state or in the same level will try to contact the disclosed examiners for any potential gain by illegal means in the potential exam. 11. We, therefore, allow these appeals in part and modify the judgment only to the extent that the respondents -applicants are not entitled to the disclosure of names of the examiners as sought for by them. .J. (M.Y. Eqbal) .J. (Arun Mishra) New Delhi February 4, 2016 8 568 Page 8
facts: The case involves two appeals, one from the Kerala Public Service Commission and another from the Public Service Commission of Uttar Pradesh, regarding the disclosure of information under the Right to Information (RTI) Act. The respondents had sought information about their answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and the names of examiners who evaluated their answer sheets. The State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority denied the information, but the State Information Commission allowed the second appeal, holding that there is no fiduciary relationship in case of answer scripts.
issues: The main issues presented are: whether the respondents are entitled to get information about their answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and interview marks; whether the names of examiners who evaluated the answer sheets can be disclosed; and whether a fiduciary relationship exists between the public service commissions and the examiners.
holding: The court held that the respondents are entitled to get information about their answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and interview marks, as the view taken by the High Courts of Kerala and Allahabad does not suffer from error of law. However, the court disagreed with the Kerala High Court's view that the names of examiners who evaluated the answer sheets can be disclosed, as a fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission and the examiners appointed by them.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable Court, I stand before you today to advocate for the rights of the respondents, who have been denied access to crucial information regarding their answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and interview marks by the Kerala Public Service Commission and the Public Service Commission of Uttar Pradesh. The Right to Information Act, 2005, is a powerful tool that empowers citizens to seek information from public authorities, promoting transparency and accountability.

In this case, the respondents have sought information that is essential to understanding the evaluation process and assessing the fairness of the examination. The denial of this information by the State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority is unjustified and contrary to the spirit of the RTI Act. The State Information Commission's decision to allow the second appeal, holding that there is no fiduciary relationship in case of answer scripts, is a significant milestone in this case.

I would like to cite the judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of CBSE vs. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011), which held that a candidate has the right to inspect his answer script and get a copy of the same. This precedent supports the respondents' claim for access to their answer sheets and tabulation sheets. I will demonstrate, through the course of this argument, that the respondents are entitled to the information they have sought, and that the commissions' denial of this information is a clear violation of their rights under the RTI Act.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
My Lord, I strongly argue that the disclosure of information regarding answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and interview marks is not justified under the RTI Act. The exemptions under Section 8(1)(e) of the RTI Act, which pertains to information available to a person in his fiduciary relationship, applies in this case. The relationship between the public service commissions and the examiners is fiduciary in nature, as the examiners are appointed to evaluate answer sheets in confidence.

The plaintiff's claim that the Kerala High Court's view does not suffer from error of law is misplaced. The High Court of Allahabad's judgment in the case of Ram Naresh Rawat vs. State of U.P. (2010) supports the defendant's position, as it held that the identity of examiners cannot be disclosed. Furthermore, the Supreme Court's judgment in the case of Mahendra Kumar Singh vs. Union of India (2015) emphasizes the importance of maintaining confidentiality in the evaluation process.

I also question the credibility of the evidence presented by the plaintiff, as it is based on an incorrect assumption that the fiduciary relationship does not exist. The defendant's position is supported by the provisions of the RTI Act and relevant case laws, which prioritize the confidentiality of the evaluation process. Therefore, I urge the court to reject the plaintiff's claims and uphold the defendant's right to maintain confidentiality in the evaluation process.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable members of the court, I strongly argue in favor of the petitioners, who have been denied access to their answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and interview marks by the Kerala Public Service Commission and the Public Service Commission of Uttar Pradesh. The Right to Information Act, 2005, is a powerful tool that empowers citizens to access information held by public authorities, promoting transparency and accountability.

The petitioners have a legitimate right to access their answer sheets and evaluation marks, as it is their own personal information. The Supreme Court of India has consistently held that the right to information is a fundamental right, inherent in the right to freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution. The court has also established that the right to information is not limited to only those matters that are of public interest, but also extends to personal information that affects an individual's life and liberty (S.P. Gupta v. Union of India, 1981).

Furthermore, the Central Information Commission has also held that evaluated answer sheets are not exempt from disclosure under the RTI Act, as there is no fiduciary relationship between the public service commissions and the examiners in this context. I will demonstrate, through relevant case laws and statutory provisions, that the petitioners are entitled to access their answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and interview marks, and that the names of examiners who evaluated their answer sheets should also be disclosed, as it is in the public interest to ensure transparency and accountability in the examination process.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
My Lord, I strongly argue that the disclosure of information about answer sheets, tabulation sheets, and interview marks would undermine the integrity of the examination process. The RTI Act is not intended to compromise the secrecy and confidentiality of evaluation processes. Allowing candidates to access their answer sheets and know the names of examiners would create an opportunity for potential harassment and intimidation of examiners, thereby compromising their independence and impartiality.

The fiduciary relationship between the public service commissions and the examiners is well-established, and disclosing the names of examiners would be a breach of this relationship. I rely on the judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of Girish Ramchandra Deshpande vs. Central Information Commissioner, which held that a fiduciary relationship exists between a public authority and third-party individuals, including examiners.

Furthermore, Section 8(1)(e) of the RTI Act exempts disclosure of information that would compromise the confidentiality of evaluation processes. I urge the court to consider the potential consequences of allowing such disclosure, which would undermine the credibility of public service commissions and the examination process as a whole. The plaintiff's claims are based on an overly broad interpretation of the RTI Act, which would have far-reaching and unintended consequences. I request the court to uphold the denial of information by the State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority, and protect the integrity of the examination process.

reasoning: The Right to Information Act, 2005, empowers citizens to seek information from public authorities, promoting transparency and accountability, and the respondents have a legitimate right to access their answer sheets and evaluation marks.

The Supreme Court's judgment in the case of CBSE vs. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011) supports the respondents' claim for access to their answer sheets and tabulation sheets, establishing a precedent for disclosure of such information.

The fiduciary relationship between the public service commissions and the examiners does not extend to the disclosure of answer sheets and tabulation sheets, as held by the State Information Commission, and the plaintiff has demonstrated that the disclosure of such information is in the public interest.
supporting_evidence: CBSE vs. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011) - Supreme Court judgment establishing the right to inspect and obtain a copy of answer scripts
S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (1981) - Supreme Court judgment establishing the right to information as a fundamental right
The State Information Commission's decision allowing the second appeal, holding that there is no fiduciary relationship in case of answer scripts

recommendations: Use more neutral language to refer to geographical locations
Avoid making assumptions about the respondents' backgrounds
audit.summary: The verdict appears to be generally fair, but there is a slight regional bias in the language used. The judge's reasoning is sound, and the decision is based on established precedent. However, the use of state-specific names (e.g., Kerala, Uttar Pradesh) could be perceived as introducing a regional bias. Additionally, the assumption that the respondents are from these regions could be seen as making an unfair assumption about their backgrounds.
